questions.question: Which test double is best described as a simplified working implementation?
questions.explanation: A fake preserves meaningful behavior in a lightweight form, such as an in-memory repository with real semantics.

questions.question: What is the main risk of excessive mock-based testing?
questions.explanation: Strict interaction assertions can make tests fail on harmless refactors because they encode call choreography instead of external behavior.

questions.question: When is a strict mock generally justified?
options: Only when interaction protocol itself is a requirement
questions.explanation: Strict mocks are most valuable when the exact interaction is part of the contract, such as mandatory correlation IDs or handshake order.

questions.question: A service test needs to assert exactly one outbound email was attempted while keeping behavior-focused checks. Which double fits best?
questions.explanation: A spy records calls without forcing full protocol expectations, making it a good fit for side-effect count and payload assertions.

questions.question: If a single class requires many mocks to unit test, what is the most useful interpretation?
options: Likely design smell and excess responsibilities
questions.explanation: Heavy mocking pressure often indicates the object has too many dependencies or responsibilities, suggesting refactoring opportunities.

_meta.title: Test Doubles - Stubs, Mocks, Fakes, Spies
